dialogs.text: ‘Another quiet night’ you thought to yourself while skimming through emails — student newsletters, club updates, random spam — when one subject line catches your eye: 🚀 JOIN THE OPEN INNOVATION HACKATHON – BEGINNERS WELCOME ON DEVPOST!
You hover over it, hesitating.

Bright colors, energetic text, and photos of smiling students greet you when you open the email. “Collaborate. Create. Innovate. Join the 48-hour Hackathon: The Byteon, hosted on Devpost! Register now. Compete remotely. Build from anywhere.”
An online hackathon? So… I don’t even have to go anywhere?
You sigh. It sounds exciting — but intimidating. Considering how these events in your mind felt like a competition of talented coders trying to one-up each other.
You scroll past it. Then scroll back. Then scroll past it again. Something about that word hackathon just sticks in your mind. Why does that keep pulling you back?
You finally give in, you sigh… and click and open the email.
Just then, your laptop’s screen flickered. The screen glitched for a second… and then a glowing icon appeared in the center, taking the form of a small, shimmering orb pulsing with blue light.
Uh… what just happened?
Hello! I see you’re reviewing an invitation to the Byteon Online Hackathon.
Whoa! Who—what are you?
Please don’t be alarmed. I’m Aira, your AI Resource Assistant. Looks like you’ve received an invitation to your first hackathon — hosted on Devpost, the official hackathon platform.
And good news: this hackathon is completely online!
She gestures, and a floating interface appears: Register Online, Join the Official Discord Server, Read the Rules, Attend the Opening Ceremony Livestream
Yeah, I guess. But… I don’t think I’m ready. I mean, I’m not a programmer or anything.

Your voice feels shaky and you feel a bit uneasy considering that you are not sure if Aira is really an AI Resource Assistant or some random malware in your computer. But given that you observed proper safety rules, it should be unrealistic to expect that this is the work of a malicious hacker.

Perhaps Aira’s rather assuring tone makes you feel at ease far more than one should expect… Uninstalling her does sound like the safest option right now as you find yourself looking at the uninstall pop-up.
Ah, there it is — the classic ‘I’m not ready’ line. Don’t worry, you’re not alone. Almost every first-timer feels that way.
No worries. Hackathons need designers, storytellers, problem-solvers, presenters, and dreamers — like you.

Before you knew it, your screen started to flash a bright neon blue light. Blinding you as your sight was robbed for a moment before the intense light faded out and you heard a few rhythmic beepings in the distance as you regained your vision.
And before you, as if you are transported into what you’d imagine cyberspace to be truly like was Aira. Smiling as she opens her arms wide like one of those tour guides.
Welcome to my digital learning space — where we’ll prepare you for the hackathon, step by step! Think of it like a simulation… with fewer bugs.
Wait, so… you’re saying anyone can join a hackathon? Even beginners like me?
Absolutely! Hackathons aren’t just for coders. They need designers, storytellers, problem-solvers, presenters, and dreamers — like you.
But I barely know where to start. It just feels… overwhelming.
That’s perfectly fine. You don’t need to know everything — you just need the courage to start learning. Think of hackathons as playgrounds for ideas, not exams.
Let me show you something.
(Aira snaps her fingers; holograms of diverse people appear — students, artists, even engineers)
Dozens of people appear in glowing scenes — some coding, others sketching, presenting, or laughing together.
See them? None of these people started as experts. Their first hackathon was full of mistakes — but also discovery, teamwork, and creativity.
So… it’s okay to fail?
Failure is a hackathoner’s secret ingredient. Every time you fail, you learn faster. Every bug you fix, every idea you pitch — it’s all experience.
Let’s break it down. Online hackathons are simple. Everything happens digitally.
A floating Register Now button pulses.
First, create your participant account. It only takes a few minutes.
That’s it? No interviews? No qualifications?
Nope. Just register, confirm eligibility, and you’re officially in.
A checklist appears: Confirm you’re not an organizer or judge, Choose Solo or Team, Agree to  intellectual property and submission requirements rules.
A large Discord icon appears.
This is where everything happens.
Channels appear floating in midair: #announcements,  #team-formation,  #help-desk, #general-chat
Need teammates? Ask in #team-formation. Confused about rules? Ask in help-desk, Stuck during coding? Ask in general-chat
So… it’s like the event headquarters?
Exactly. Think of Discord as the hackathon’s digital campus.
Every hackathon has rules — submission format, deadlines, judging criteria.
Words highlight automatically: Project must be built during the event, Submit before deadline, Follow theme guidelines.
Reading the rules isn’t scary. It’s strategic.
A virtual stage forms. Livestream countdown appears: 00:12:45 until Opening Ceremony
This is where organizers explain the theme, prizes, timeline, and sponsor challenges.
Clips flash: Hosts welcoming participants, Mentors introducing themselves, Prize announcements.
It sets the tone. And trust me — the energy is contagious
Tell me, what stops you from joining?
She leans forward slightly, gesturing to you to feel free to speak further about what is truly on your mind.

choices.text: I’m afraid my ideas won’t be good.

dialogs.text: Skills can be learned — that’s the easiest part. What’s harder is believing that you can learn. Let’s work on that first.
No idea is born perfect! Great ideas come from asking questions, not knowing answers. The fact that you care already means your ideas matter.

And even if your idea is flawed, it doesn’t mean it’s not open to improvement or reiteration. That’s one of the greatest ways to innovate a concept that may not seem to work but does in the right conditions.
Ah, the fear of going solo! Fun fact: hackathons are designed for strangers to meet. You’ll find teammates who complement your strengths — trust me.
Okay, first challenge! Let’s clear your mind of myths about hackathons. Think of it as your warm-up quest.
Let’s see if you’re ready.
It’s mostly straightforward. Let’s take it step by step — like a game.
Like… a game?
Exactly. You’ll be surprised how much fun learning can be when it feels like a hack.
MiniGame: “Hackathon Myth Buster” — Aira throws statements and you decide True or False.

minigame.description: Aira gives you statements—decide if they’re True or False to earn Confidence Points.

questions.tip: Hackathons need designers, storytellers, presenters, researchers—every skill matters!
questions.aira_reaction: Right! Hackathons thrive on diverse skill sets!

questions.statement: You must know advanced coding before joining.
questions.tip: Beginner-friendly hackathons expect learners. You learn faster by doing!
resources: No-Code Tools: Glide, Bubble, Figma Prototype Mode
questions.aira_reaction: Exactly! Everyone starts somewhere—even expert hackers began as beginners!

questions.tip: A simple prototype or even a mockup is enough—as long as the idea shines.

questions.statement: If my idea isn’t completely original, I shouldn’t join.
questions.tip: Execution beats originality. Even similar ideas can shine when improved or personalized.
questions.aira_reaction: Nice! Innovation is about improving what's already there!

questions.statement: Hackathons are overly competitive and toxic.
questions.tip: Most hackathons are friendly, supportive, and focused on learning.
questions.aira_reaction: Correct! Hackathons are more about community than competition!

questions.statement: You must stay awake for 24–48 hours straight.
questions.tip: Smart hackers rest. Creativity collapses when your brain does.
questions.aira_reaction: Exactly! Rested minds create the best hacks!

questions.statement: Hackathons are about teamwork and creativity.
questions.tip: Strong communication beats strong code. Teams that talk win more.
questions.aira_reaction: Absolutely! Collaboration is the ultimate hackathon power!

questions.tip: Hackathons welcome everyone—students, professionals, hobbyists, and beginners!
questions.aira_reaction: Yes! Hackathons are for ALL curious minds!

questions.statement: You need expensive equipment or top-tier devices.
questions.tip: Hackathons judge ideas and teamwork, not your hardware.
questions.aira_reaction: Correct! Creativity doesn't need high specs!

questions.statement: If you don’t win, you wasted your time.
questions.tip: Every hackathon levels you up—skills, friends, confidence, portfolio pieces.
resources: How to Write a Hackathon Case Study
questions.aira_reaction: Exactly! Experience is the REAL prize!

achievement.title: Hackathon Myth Buster
achievement.description: Completed the first mini-game by busting common hackathon myths. Confidence boosted!

dialogs.text: Excellent! You busted all the myths like a pro.
The virtual space fades back into your room.
You’ve earned your first achievement!
Heh, not bad. Maybe this hackathon thing isn’t so scary after all.
You look at the email it is still open.
Two buttons glow at the bottom: Register Now and Close Tab
Discord invite link pulses underneath. Opening Ceremony countdown continues ticking.
You don’t need to be ready. You just need to begin.
Your cursor hovers over the register button. This time… your hesitation feels different. Less fear. More anticipation.
That’s the spirit! Remember — the hardest part isn’t joining… it’s deciding to try.
Tomorrow, we’ll talk about teams. Because even online… no one hacks alone.

You look at the uninstall pop-up again… it’s right there waiting to be clicked but you feel hesitant pressing it. As if this might be something that would give you the confidence boost you need. Against your better judgement, you close the window. You can uninstall her later if she really is malicious… right?

achievement.title: First Steps
achievement.description: Complete Chapter 1 of How to Hackathon
